# Scaffolding and AI in Education

## Introduction

Structured scaffolding improves student outcomes when working with AI tools (Kasneci et al. 102274). Active dialogue with AI produces stronger learning than passive delegation (Mollick and Mollick 1). This aligns with the conversational framework for learning (Laurillard 15).

Research identifies "assignment delegators" as a distinct behaviour type (Stojanov et al. 3). Behavioural nudging offers a low-cost intervention (Thaler and Sunstein 6). The zone of proximal development provides the theoretical foundation (Vygotsky 86).

## Works Cited

Kasneci, Enkelejda, et al. "ChatGPT for Good? On Opportunities and Challenges of Large Language Models for Education." *Learning and Individual Differences*, vol. 103, 2023, 102274.

Laurillard, Diana. *Rethinking University Teaching: A Conversational Framework for the Effective Use of Learning Technologies.* 2nd ed., RoutledgeFalmer, 2002.

Mollick, Ethan R., and Lilach Mollick. "Assigning AI: Seven Approaches for Students, with Prompts." *The Wharton School Research Paper*, 2023.

Stojanov, Aleksandar, Nathalie Grønbæk, and Murat Kulahci. "Can We Delegate Learning to Automation? A Comparative Study of LLM Chatbots, Search Engines, and Books." *arXiv Preprint*, 2024.

Thaler, Richard H., and Cass R. Sunstein. *Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness.* Yale University Press, 2008.

Vygotsky, Lev S. *Mind in Society: The Development of Higher Psychological Processes.* Harvard University Press, 1978.
